q: Concentric metallic hollow spheres of radii R and 4R hold charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. Given that surface charge densities of the concentric spheres are equal, the potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is:
question: Concentric metallic hollow spheres of radii R and 4R hold charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. Given that surface charge densities of the concentric spheres are equal, the potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is:
solution:
As given the surface charge density for both the spheres is same, hence

     σ1=σ2∴Q1A1=Q2A2⇒Q14πR2=Q24π4R2∴Q2=16Q1

From above diagram, the electric potential for both the spheres is 

VInner=KQ1R+KQ24R=KRQ1+16Q14=5KQ1R.

In the same way for outer sphere, 

VOuter=KQ14R+KQ24R=K4R16Q1+Q1=17KQ14R.

Now the potential difference is,

∆V=Vinner-Vouter

=5KQ1R-17KQ14R=3Q116πε0R.

⇒q=mgE=0.1×10-3×9.84.9×105=2.0×10-9C

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: In electrostatics, a conductor does not store any net charge inside.

Reason R: Inside the capacitor (with no dielectric medium), the free charge carriers, if placed between the plates of capacitor, experience force and drift.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: In electrostatics, a conductor does not store any net charge inside.

Reason R: Inside the capacitor (with no dielectric medium), the free charge carriers, if placed between the plates of capacitor, experience force and drift.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
In electrostatics, the electric field inside a conductor is zero. According to Gauss's law, $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{s} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{\epsilon_0}$. Since $\vec{E} = 0$ everywhere inside the conductor, the net charge enclosed $q_{in}$ must be zero. Thus, a conductor does not store any net charge inside its bulk, and any excess charge resides only on its surface. Assertion A is true.

Inside a capacitor with no dielectric medium, a uniform electric field exists between the plates. If free charge carriers are placed in this region, they will experience an electrostatic force $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$ and will drift towards the respective oppositely charged plates. Reason R is true.

Although both statements are factually correct, the behavior of charge carriers in the electric field of a capacitor does not explain why the net charge inside a conductor is zero in electrostatics. Therefore, both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.

Answer: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

subject: Physics
q: An infinitely long positively charged straight thread has a linear charge density $\lambda \mathrm{Cm}^{-1}$. An electron revolves along a circular path having axis along the length of the wire. The graph that correctly represents the variation of the kinetic energy of electron as a function of radius of circular path from the wire is :
question: An infinitely long positively charged straight thread has a linear charge density $\lambda \mathrm{Cm}^{-1}$. An electron revolves along a circular path having axis along the length of the wire. The graph that correctly represents the variation of the kinetic energy of electron as a function of radius of circular path from the wire is :
solution:
Electric field $\mathrm{E}$ at a distance $\mathrm{r}$ due to infinite long wire is $E=\frac{2 k \lambda}{r}$ Force of electron $\Rightarrow F=e E$ $\begin{aligned} & F=e\left(\frac{2 k \lambda}{r}\right) \\ & F=\frac{2 k \lambda e}{r} \end{aligned}$
This force will provide required centripetal force $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{F} & =\frac{\mathrm{m}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ \mathrm{v} & =\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}} \\ \mathrm{KE} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are separated with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and placed in a medium of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. What will be the equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force?
question: If two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are separated with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and placed in a medium of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. What will be the equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force?
solution:
The force between the two charges when they are placed in dielectric is

F1=14πεokq1q2 d2

The force between the two charges when they are placed at separation d' is

F2=14πεoq1q2 d'2 

Since, the force between the two charges is same in both case

F1=F2

⇒14πεok     q1q2 d2= 14πεo q1q2 d'2

⇒d'=dk

subject: Physics
q: Electric field in a region is given by $\vec{E}=A x \hat{i}+B y \hat{j}$, where $A=10 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ and $B=5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. If the electric potential at a point $(10,20)$ is 500 V, then the electric potential at origin is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
question: Electric field in a region is given by $\vec{E}=A x \hat{i}+B y \hat{j}$, where $A=10 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ and $B=5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. If the electric potential at a point $(10,20)$ is 500 V, then the electric potential at origin is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
solution:
The electric field is related to potential by $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.
Given: $\vec{E} = Ax\hat{i} + By\hat{j}$ with $A = 10$ V/m² and $B = 5$ V/m²
This means:
$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -Ax$ and $\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = -By$
Integrating:
$V(x,y) = -\frac{Ax^2}{2} - \frac{By^2}{2} + C = -5x^2 - \frac{5y^2}{2} + C$
Using the boundary condition at $(10, 20)$ where $V = 500$ V:
$500 = -5(100) - \frac{5(400)}{2} + C$
$500 = -500 - 1000 + C$
$C = 2000$ V
At the origin $(0, 0)$:
$V(0,0) = -5(0) - \frac{5(0)}{2} + 2000 = 2000$ V

subject: Physics
q:
Statement-I : A point charge is brought in an electric field. The value of electric field at a point near to the charge may increase if the charge is positive.

Statement-II : An electric dipole is placed in a uniform electric field. The net electric force on the dipole will not be zero.
question:
Statement-I : A point charge is brought in an electric field. The value of electric field at a point near to the charge may increase if the charge is positive.

Statement-II : An electric dipole is placed in a uniform electric field. The net electric force on the dipole will not be zero.
options: Both statement-I and statement-II are true.
Both statement-I and statement-II are false.
Statement-I is true but statement-II is false.
Statement-I is false but statement-II is true.
solution:
If a positive point charge is brought in an electric field. The value of electric field at a point near to the charge may increase or decreases. Hence, statement I is true.

An electric dipole experiences zero net force in a uniform electric field. Hence, statement II is false.

solution:
As electric field inside the charged conducting sphere is zero, therefore potential will be constant for the inside part.

For outside part, electric potential decreases as V=kqr and the conducting sphere behaves like a point charge concentrated at the centre.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason(R)
Assertion (A) : Work done by electric field on moving a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.
Reason (R) : Electric lines of forces are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason(R)
Assertion (A) : Work done by electric field on moving a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.
Reason (R) : Electric lines of forces are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
The equipotential surfaces are surfaces where the electric potential is the same at every point. In other words, there is no potential difference along an equipotential surface. Since work done (W) is given by the equation W=q∆V, and there is no potential difference on an equipotential surface, the work done is always zero.

As there is no force along the surface of the equipotential surface, the electric field must be perpendicular to the surface.

Hence, while both statements are correct, the reason (R) is providing the correct explanation for why the work done on a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.

At what location on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis should a point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> be placed so that the net electric field is zero at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis ?

At what location on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis should a point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> be placed so that the net electric field is zero at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis ?
solution:
Let the charge 40 μC be placed at a distance X0 from the origin as shown in the figure.

For the electric field at x=2 to be 0,

EP=0⇒k×1022=k×40 X0-22

⇒12=2X0-2

⇒X0-2=4

Therefore, the charge is located at X0=4+2=6 cm

question: Three infinitely long charged thin sheets are placed as shown in figure. The magnitude of electric field at the point $P$ is $\frac{x \sigma}{\epsilon_o}$. The value of $x$ is _______ (all quantities are measured in SI units).
solution: $\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{p}} & =\left(\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{2 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}\right)(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \\ & =-\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \hat{\mathrm{i}}\end{aligned}$

q: Expression for an electric field is given by E→=4000 x2 i^ V m-1. The electric flux through the cube of side 20 cm when placed in electric field (as shown in the figure) is ______ V cm.
question: Expression for an electric field is given by E→=4000 x2 i^ V m-1. The electric flux through the cube of side 20 cm when placed in electric field (as shown in the figure) is ______ V cm.
solution:
Electric flux is expressed as ϕ=E→·A→=EAcosθ, here, A is area of the surface and θ is the angle between the electric field lines and the normal to A.

The flux will be only from DEFG surface as on the surface OABC field is zero and for rest of the surface, area vector is perpendicular to field. 

So, ϕ=EA

=4000×(0.2)2×(0.2)2 

=4000×16×10-4 V m

=640 V cm

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass 1 mg and charge q is lying at the mid-point of two stationary particles kept at a distance 2 m when each is carrying same charge q. If the free charged particle is displaced from its equilibrium position through distance x x<<1 m. The particle executes SHM. Its angular frequency of oscillation will be _______ ×105 rad s-1 (if q2=10C2)
question: A particle of mass 1 mg and charge q is lying at the mid-point of two stationary particles kept at a distance 2 m when each is carrying same charge q. If the free charged particle is displaced from its equilibrium position through distance x x<<1 m. The particle executes SHM. Its angular frequency of oscillation will be _______ ×105 rad s-1 (if q2=10C2)
solution:
Net force on free charged particle, 

F=kq2d+x2-kq2d-x2

F=-kq24dxd2-x22

a=-4kq2dmxd4

a=-4kq2md3x

So, angular frequency 

ω=4 kq2md3

ω=4×9×109×101×10-6×13

ω=6×108 rad sec-1

q: The vehicles carrying inflammable fluids usually have metallic chains touching the ground :
question: The vehicles carrying inflammable fluids usually have metallic chains touching the ground :
options: To protect tyres from catching dirt from ground
To conduct excess charge due to air friction to ground and prevent sparking
solution: Static charge is developed due to air friction. This can result in combustion. So, metallic chains is used to discharge excess charge.

subject: Physics
q: A charge $Q$ is placed at a distance $\mathrm{a} / 2$ above the centre of the square surface of edge a as shown in the figure. The electric flux through the square surface is:
question: A charge $Q$ is placed at a distance $\mathrm{a} / 2$ above the centre of the square surface of edge a as shown in the figure. The electric flux through the square surface is:
solution:
When cube is of side a and point charge $Q$ is at the center of the cube then the total electric flux due to this charge will pass evenly through the six faces of the cube. So, the electric flux through one face will be equal to $1 / 6$ of the total electric flux due to this charge.
Flux through 6 faces $=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_0}$
$\therefore$ Flux through 1 face, $=\frac{Q}{6 \epsilon_0}$

V1-V2=-∫E.dx
=-∫-51ax+b dx
=-ax22+bx-51
= -a2+b-a×252-5b
= -202+10-250+50
=180 V

subject: Physics
q: There are three co-centric conducting spherical shells $A, B$ and $C$ of radii $a, b$ and $c$ respectively $(c>b>a)$ and they are charged with charge $q_{1}, q_{2}$ and $q_{3}$ respectively. The potentials of the spheres $A, B$ and $C$ respectively, are :
question: There are three co-centric conducting spherical shells $A, B$ and $C$ of radii $a, b$ and $c$ respectively $(c>b>a)$ and they are charged with charge $q_{1}, q_{2}$ and $q_{3}$ respectively. The potentials of the spheres $A, B$ and $C$ respectively, are :
solution:
The potential at any point due to a charged spherical shell of radius $R$ and charge $Q$ is $V = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_o} \frac{Q}{R}$ for points inside or on the surface ($r \le R$), and $V = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_o} \frac{Q}{r}$ for points outside ($r > R$).
Let $k = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_o}$. The potentials of the three shells $A, B, C$ with radii $a < b < c$ and charges $q_1, q_2, q_3$ are calculated by superposition.
For shell $A$ ($r = a$):
Shell $A$ is on its own surface, while it is inside shells $B$ and $C$.
$V_A = k\frac{q_1}{a} + k\frac{q_2}{b} + k\frac{q_3}{c} = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_o} \left( \frac{q_1}{a} + \frac{q_2}{b} + \frac{q_3}{c} \right)$
For shell $B$ ($r = b$):
Shell $B$ is outside shell $A$, on its own surface, and inside shell $C$.
$V_B = k\frac{q_1}{b} + k\frac{q_2}{b} + k\frac{q_3}{c} = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_o} \left( \frac{q_1 + q_2}{b} + \frac{q_3}{c} \right)$
For shell $C$ ($r = c$):
Shell $C$ is outside shells $A$ and $B$, and on its own surface.
$V_C = k\frac{q_1}{c} + k\frac{q_2}{c} + k\frac{q_3}{c} = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_o} \left( \frac{q_1 + q_2 + q_3}{c} \right)$
Comparing these expressions with the given options, option (3) matches correctly.

subject: Physics
q:
A 1 m long copper wire carries a current of 1 A. If the cross section of the wire is 2.0 mm2 and the resistivity of copper is 1.7×10-8 Ω m. The force experienced by moving electron in the wire is _____×10-23 N

(Charge of electron=1.6×10-19C )
question:
A 1 m long copper wire carries a current of 1 A. If the cross section of the wire is 2.0 mm2 and the resistivity of copper is 1.7×10-8 Ω m. The force experienced by moving electron in the wire is _____×10-23 N

(Charge of electron=1.6×10-19C )
solution:
Given here, length l=1 m, current i=1 A, area A=2×10-6 m and resistivity ρ=1.7×10-8 Ω m.

Resistance of wire is R=ρlA=1.7×10-8×12×10-5=1.72×10-2 Ω

Now, electric field in the wire is E=ρJ, where, J=iA is current density.

Thus, E=iρA=iR=1×1.72×10-2 V m-1

Electric force in terms of electric field is given by F=qE

Therefore, force experienced by moving electron is  F=1.6×10-19×1.72×10-2

=1.36×10-21=136×10-23 N

At a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math>, the resultant electric field is zero. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is :
question: Two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>q</mi></math> are separated by a distance ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>’ in air. At a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math>, the resultant electric field is zero. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is :
solution:
Given: E→netP=0

⇒kqx2=k×3qr-x2

⇒r-x2=3x2

⇒r-x=3x

⇒x=r3+1

subject: Physics
q:
Two point charges of 1 nC and 2 nC are placed at the two corners of equilateral triangle of side 3 cm. The work done in bringing a charge of 3 nC from infinity to the third corner of the triangle is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
$\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$
question:
Two point charges of 1 nC and 2 nC are placed at the two corners of equilateral triangle of side 3 cm. The work done in bringing a charge of 3 nC from infinity to the third corner of the triangle is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
$\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$
solution:
Two point charges of 1 nC and 2 nC are placed at two corners of an equilateral triangle with side 3 cm. The work done in bringing a 3 nC charge from infinity to the third corner equals the charge times the potential at that point.
Both source charges are at distance 0.03 m from the third corner.
The potential due to the 1 nC charge is $V_1 = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{0.03} = 300$ V
and due to the 2 nC charge is $V_2 = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}}{0.03} = 600$ V.
Total potential: $V_{total} = 300 + 600 = 900$ V
Work done: $W = 3 \times 10^{-9} \times 900 = 2.7 \times 10^{-6}$ J = 2.7 μJ

A charged particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> being suspended through a silk thread of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and remain stayed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the wall. Then the charge on the particle will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________ [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: Suppose a uniformly charged wall provides a uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> normally. A charged particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> being suspended through a silk thread of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and remain stayed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the wall. Then the charge on the particle will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________ [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
As we can see from geometry, sinθ=1020=12

⇒θ=30°

Vertical component of tension will balance the weight and the horizontal component will balance the electric force. Therefore,

tanθ=qEmg

⇒tan30°=q×2×1042×10-3×10

⇒13=q×106

⇒q=13×10-6 C=13 μC

Hence, x=3.

question: The distance between charges +q and -q is 2l and between +2q and -2q is 4l. The electrostatic potential at point P at a distance r from centre O is -αqlr2×109 V, where the value of α is ______. (Use 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2)
solution:
Considering two dipoles with charges 2q & q, we can write the equivalent setup as shown below:

Therefore, net dipole moment is Pnet=6ql in the leftward direction.

Potential required, 

V=Kp→·r→r3=9×1096qlr2cos120o

=−27qlr2×109

Therefore, α=27.
